title: Authentication & Authorization — System Design
description: Designing the identity layer for a platform: prove who a user is (authentication) and decide what they may do (authorization), securely and at scale. The interesting problems are security-first: storing credentials safely, the sessions-vs-tokens tradeoff, federated login via OAuth/OIDC, token refresh and revocation, an authorization model (RBAC/ABAC), MFA, and resisting credential-stuffing. We build from a password login toward a federated, token-based, revocable, MFA-protected identity system.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: API Gateway: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Audit Log
nodes.description: Audit Log: Telemetry, monitoring, alerting, logging, dashboard, or audit component.

nodes.label: Auth Service
nodes.description: Auth Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Authorization Service
nodes.description: Authorization Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Client App
nodes.description: Client App: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: External IdP — OAuth/OIDC
nodes.description: External IdP — OAuth/OIDC: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Signing Keys / JWKS
nodes.description: Signing Keys / JWKS: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Login Rate Limiter
nodes.description: Login Rate Limiter: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: MFA Service
nodes.description: MFA Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: SMS / TOTP / Push
nodes.description: SMS / TOTP / Push: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Policy / Roles Store
nodes.description: Policy / Roles Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Resource Service
nodes.description: Resource Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Revocation List
nodes.description: Revocation List: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Session Store
nodes.description: Session Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Token Service
nodes.description: Token Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: User / Credential Store
nodes.description: User / Credential Store: Authoritative or operational state store for records, metadata, counters, locks, or durable state.

functional: Register and authenticate users with a password.
Issue a credential (session or token) after successful login.
Support federated login via external identity providers (OAuth/OIDC).
Authorize requests against a permission model (roles/attributes).
Support MFA and let users manage sessions/devices.
Log out / revoke credentials, individually and en masse.
nonFunctional: Never store passwords recoverably; resist database theft.
Verify credentials on every request with minimal latency (sub-ms ideally).
Revocation must be honored promptly even with stateless tokens.
Resist credential stuffing, brute force, and token theft/replay.
Auditable: security-relevant events are recorded immutably.
Scale verification to the whole platform's request rate.

capacity.label: Verification QPS
capacity.note: Every authenticated request checks a token — the hot path. A local signature verify is ~10–50µs of CPU, so 100k/s is a few cores spread across the fleet — only because it's NOT a central call.

capacity.label: Login QPS
capacity.note: Logins are orders of magnitude rarer than verifications. With ~50M users and a few logins/user/day plus morning peaks, peak login is ~1k/s — the basis for sizing the slow-hash tier.

capacity.label: Password-hash CPU
capacity.note: Argon2id/bcrypt is tuned to ~50–100ms. At 1k logins/s that's ~50–100 hashing-core-seconds/s ≈ 50–100 cores just for hashing. So login must be separately rate-limited and horizontally scaled, and kept off the per-request path.

capacity.label: Refresh QPS
capacity.note: Roughly one refresh per access-token TTL (~15 min) per active session. A few thousand/s, each a denylist check + a rotation write — small but stateful.

capacity.label: MFA challenge QPS
capacity.note: Only MFA accounts, only at login/step-up. Hundreds/s; SMS/push add an external-provider dependency and cost per send.

capacity.label: Authorization-decision QPS
capacity.note: Up to ~100k/s if every request authorizes, but short-TTL decision caching collapses this to the cache-miss rate; only sensitive actions force a fresh policy lookup.

capacity.label: Audit write volume
capacity.note: Login success/fail, MFA, refresh rotate/reuse, logout, role/policy change, suspicious login, key rotation. Dominated by login attempts (incl. failures under attack); append-only sink sized for burst, retained months+.

capacity.label: Denylist / revocation memory
capacity.note: Only revoked-but-unexpired tokens need entries. With ~15-min access TTL, live revocations number in the thousands — a few MB, replicated close to verifiers.

capacity.label: Rate-limit counter cardinality
capacity.note: Counters keyed per account, IP, subnet, tenant, and device. Cardinality scales with active attackers/users but each key auto-expires, so the fast store stays bounded.

capacity.label: Revocation latency
capacity.note: Short access TTL + denylist make a revoked credential stop working within seconds, not its full lifetime.

api.description: Authenticate with username + password. Rate-limited. For a non-MFA account, returns full tokens; for an MFA account, returns only a limited mfa_pending_token + challenge_id — full tokens are issued only after /v1/mfa/verify.
api.response: 200 OK { "access_token": "...", "refresh_token": "...", "expires_in": 900 }  // or, if MFA enabled: 200 OK { "mfa_required": true, "challenge_id": "chl_...", "mfa_pending_token": "...", "factors": ["totp", "webauthn"] }

api.description: Begin a second-factor challenge for a login that returned mfa_required. Sends a code/push or returns a WebAuthn challenge. Authenticated by the limited mfa_pending_token, not a full session.
api.response: 200 OK { "challenge_id": "chl_...", "factor": "webauthn", "webauthn_options": { ... } }  // for code factors: 202 Accepted (code delivered out-of-band)

api.description: Verify the second factor and, only on success, issue full access + refresh tokens. Failed verifications are rate-limited and audited; the mfa_pending_token alone never grants resource access.
Enroll a new second factor (TOTP seed, WebAuthn credential, or phone) for the current user. Returns backup codes once on first enrollment. Requires a fully authenticated (post-MFA) session.
Remove an enrolled factor. Sensitive: requires step-up re-authentication and is audited.
Exchange a refresh token for a new access token; rotates the refresh token.
Revoke the current session/refresh token (and optionally all sessions).

Internal decision: may this subject perform this action on this resource? A POST carries full context — tenant, resource owner, and ABAC attributes — so RBAC and ABAC rules can both be evaluated. The response echoes the policy_version it was decided under and a cache TTL; callers may cache the decision until ttl_ms elapses or that policy_version is invalidated.

dataModel.name: users
dataModel.note: Identity + password hash. The hash is salted and slow-hashed; the plaintext never stored.

dataModel.name: refresh_token_families
dataModel.note: One row per issued refresh token, grouped into a family (one login/device). Rotation retires the old row and links the new one via rotated_from; replay of a retired token in a family triggers revocation of the whole family. This is the unit of revocation and reuse detection.

fields.name: token_hash
fields.type: bytea (hash of the refresh token; raw value never stored)

fields.name: rotated_from
fields.type: uuid null (previous token_id in the chain)

dataModel.name: mfa_factors
dataModel.note: Enrolled second factors per user. TOTP seeds are stored encrypted (KMS-wrapped); WebAuthn stores only the credential id + public key (no secret). Backup codes are hashed.

fields.name: secret_ref
fields.type: varchar null (KMS key ref for encrypted TOTP seed)

dataModel.name: oauth_login_attempts
dataModel.note: Transient OIDC login state, bound to one authorization-code flow. Single-use: consumed_at is set on callback so state/nonce/PKCE can't be replayed. Short TTL.

dataModel.name: roles_permissions
dataModel.note: RBAC/ABAC model: principals -> roles -> permissions, scoped by tenant. policy_version bumps invalidate cached decisions; attribute_rules carries ABAC conditions (owner, environment, time).

dataModel.name: rate_limit_counters
dataModel.note: Sliding/fixed-window counters for abuse defense, keyed by dimension. Lives in a fast replicated store (e.g. Redis) with TTL expiry, not the primary DB. Multiple dimensions so distributed stuffing can't dodge a single counter.

fields.name: key
fields.type: varchar PK (dimension:value, e.g. account:usr_1, ip:1.2.3.4, subnet:1.2.3/24, tenant:t_9, device:dev_3)

dataModel.name: identity_links
dataModel.note: Maps external IdP identities (OAuth/OIDC sub) to local users.

dataModel.name: audit_log
dataModel.note: Append-only, immutable record of every security-relevant event (login success/failure, MFA challenge/verify, refresh rotate/reuse, logout, role/policy change, suspicious login, key rotation). Write-once storage, restricted read access, retained months+ for incident response and compliance. Correlation_id ties events across a session/request chain.

fields.name: event_type
fields.type: varchar (login_success | login_fail | mfa_verify | refresh_reuse | role_change | key_rotation | ...)

patterns.name: Slow password hashing (salt + argon2/bcrypt)
patterns.what: Store a per-user salted hash from a deliberately slow KDF; never the plaintext.
patterns.group: Naive Baseline: Password Login & Secure Storage

patterns.name: Sessions vs stateless tokens
patterns.what: Server-side session id (lookup per request) vs self-contained signed token (verify by signature).
patterns.whenToUse: Choose by revocation needs vs verification scale.

patterns.name: OAuth / OIDC federation
patterns.what: Delegate authentication to an external IdP and map its identity to a local user.
patterns.whenToUse: Social/enterprise login without handling others' credentials.

patterns.name: Short access + rotating refresh tokens
patterns.what: Issue short-lived access tokens renewed by a long-lived, rotating refresh token.
patterns.whenToUse: Balancing stateless verification speed against revocation.

patterns.name: Token revocation (denylist / short TTL)
patterns.what: Make stateless tokens revocable via a denylist and short lifetimes.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever you need to invalidate a credential before it expires.

patterns.name: RBAC / ABAC authorization
patterns.what: Decide access from roles and/or attributes, evaluated by a policy service.

patterns.name: Multi-factor authentication
patterns.what: Require a second factor (TOTP/push/SMS) beyond the password.

patterns.name: Rate limiting / abuse detection
patterns.what: Throttle and lock out brute-force and credential-stuffing attempts.

patterns.name: Key rotation (JWKS)
patterns.what: Rotate signing keys and publish public keys so verifiers fetch the current set.

steps.title: 1. Naive Baseline: Password Login & Secure Storage
steps.description: Start with the simplest thing that works: a user registers a password, you store it, and on login you check the submitted password and hand back a credential so they're 'logged in'. That naive version is correct — and it immediately forces the one decision that, gotten wrong, makes nothing else matter: how the password is stored. So the baseline is really about credential storage. Never store the plaintext (or a fast hash); store only a per-user salted, slow-hashed (argon2/bcrypt) digest, and return generic errors. This naive login motivates everything that follows — it issues a credential but says nothing about how that credential is verified per request, revoked, federated, or protected from abuse.
steps.decisionPrompt: Your user database is stolen. How bad is it, and what determines that?

concepts.definition: A per-user random salt mixed into the hash so identical passwords differ and rainbow tables fail.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without salt, one precomputed table cracks everyone.
concepts.group: Naive Baseline: Password Login & Secure Storage

concepts.definition: A deliberately expensive hash so guessing is costly.
concepts.whyItMatters: Fast hashes (SHA-256) let attackers try billions/sec.
concepts.group: Naive Baseline: Password Login & Secure Storage

talkingPoints: State that credential storage is the whole ballgame for the baseline.
Name salting + a slow KDF and generic error messages.

traps.why: A DB breach exposes or trivially cracks every password.
traps.instead: Salt + a slow KDF (argon2id/bcrypt) tuned to a high work factor.

traps.trap: Returning whether the username or the password was wrong
traps.why: It lets attackers enumerate valid accounts.
traps.instead: Return a single generic 'invalid credentials' for both.

bottlenecks.issue: Verifying a password on every request would be far too slow (slow KDF by design).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Issue a session/token after login so requests don't re-hash (next steps).

strong: Salts + slow-hashes and uses generic errors.

whyNow: Secure credential storage is the precondition for everything above it.

recap.after: Users authenticate with a password stored only as a per-user salted, slow-hashed digest; login returns a credential.
recap.newRisk: Re-hashing on every request is far too slow, and the credential we just issued has no verification, revocation, federation, or abuse story yet.

flows.name: Login -> verify -> issue token
flows.note: The slow hash runs once at login (not per request); valid credentials yield short access + refresh tokens, invalid ones a single generic 401.

view.caption: The client app sends register/login to the Auth Service, which writes a salted slow hash to the User / Credential Store.

steps.title: 2. Sessions vs Stateless Tokens
steps.description: After login, issue a credential the client presents on each request. Two models: a server-side session id looked up per request, or a self-contained signed token (JWT) verified by signature. The choice trades easy revocation against verification scale.
steps.decisionPrompt: Do you look up a session on every request, or verify a self-contained token? What does each cost you?

concepts.definition: An opaque id mapping to session data in a store; looked up per request.
concepts.whyItMatters: Trivially revocable (delete the row) but adds a lookup to every request.

concepts.definition: A signed claims blob verified by signature, no lookup.
concepts.whyItMatters: Verifies fast and scales, but is hard to revoke before expiry.

options.name: Stateless access tokens (default, with revocation later)
options.description: The client carries a signed JWT whose claims any service verifies by checking the signature against the public key, with no per-request store lookup.
pros: No per-request store lookup — scales to the whole platform.
Self-contained claims usable across services.
cons: Hard to revoke before expiry (addressed with short TTL + denylist).
Token size and key management.

view.caption: The API Gateway verifies a self-contained token at the Token Service, which validates the signature against the Signing Keys / JWKS, with no store lookup.

options.name: Server-side sessions
options.description: Login creates a session record in a shared store and hands the client an opaque id; the gateway looks that id up on every request.
pros: Revoke instantly by deleting the session.
Small opaque cookie; server holds the data.
cons: A store lookup on every request becomes a hot dependency.
Sticky/shared session store to scale.

view.caption: The API Gateway resolves each request's opaque id by looking it up in the Session Store.

traps.trap: Putting sensitive data in a JWT thinking it's hidden
traps.why: JWT payloads are signed, not encrypted — anyone can read them.
traps.instead: Only put non-sensitive claims; encrypt or keep secrets server-side.

traps.trap: Long-lived stateless tokens with no revocation plan
traps.why: A stolen token works until it expires, with no way to kill it.
traps.instead: Short access-token TTL + a revocation mechanism (next steps).

failureDrills.scenario: A user's access token is stolen.
failureDrills.mitigation: Short TTL limits the window; refresh rotation + denylist cut it off (next step).

strong: Articulates the revocation-vs-scale tradeoff and picks deliberately.

recap.before: Login handed back a credential, but its form (and how it's verified per request) was left undecided.
recap.after: Requests carry a session or token verified without re-hashing — the model chosen deliberately.
recap.newRisk: Stateless tokens are hard to revoke — the central tension to resolve.

whyNow: The credential model shapes verification scale and revocation for everything after.

view.caption: The client presents a credential to the API Gateway, which either verifies its signature at the Token Service or looks it up in the Session Store.

steps.title: 3. Federated Login (OAuth / OIDC)
steps.description: Let users log in via an external identity provider (Google, Okta) using OAuth 2.0 / OIDC: redirect to the IdP, exchange an authorization code for an id token, verify it, and map the external identity to a local user. You never see the user's IdP password.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why use the authorization-code flow rather than just having the IdP hand you a token directly?

concepts.definition: Redirect to IdP -> code -> server exchanges code for tokens.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps tokens off the front channel/URL; the secret exchange is server-to-server.

concepts.definition: A signed token from the IdP asserting the user's identity.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you authenticate without ever handling the user's IdP credentials.

flows.name: Authorization-code login
flows.note: Code is exchanged server-side; the id token is verified before trusting it.

traps.trap: Using the implicit flow (token in the redirect URL)
traps.why: Tokens leak via browser history, referrers, and logs.
traps.instead: Use the authorization-code flow (with PKCE for public clients).

traps.trap: Trusting the id token without verifying signature, issuer, audience, and nonce
traps.why: A forged or replayed token would authenticate an attacker.
traps.instead: Validate signature against the IdP's JWKS plus issuer/audience/nonce/expiry.

failureDrills.scenario: An attacker replays a stolen id token to your callback.
failureDrills.mitigation: Verify nonce/state and expiry; bind the token to the login attempt.

strong: Uses code flow + PKCE and fully validates the id token.
Maps external sub to a local user.
weak: Trusts the id token without validation.

recap.after: Federated login via OAuth/OIDC, mapped to local users, without handling their IdP password.
recap.newRisk: Token validation must be airtight; account linking adds edge cases.

whyNow: Federation is a core requirement and reuses the token machinery just built.

view.caption: The client app logs in with Google through the Auth Service, which redirects to the external IdP, exchanges the returned code for an id token, and maps the identity in the User / Credential Store.

options.name: Authorization-code flow (default)
options.description: The IdP redirects back with a one-time code; the Auth service exchanges it server-to-server for an id token, validates it, and maps the identity to a local user.
pros: The access/ID token is exchanged server-to-server over a back channel, never exposed in the browser URL or history.
A short-lived one-time code in the redirect limits the blast radius if it leaks.
PKCE lets even public clients (SPA, mobile) use the code flow safely without a client secret.
cons: Extra round trips: redirect to IdP, code back, then a server-side code-for-token exchange.
Requires a registered redirect URI and back-channel call, so more moving parts than a direct grant.
Your Auth service must hold the IdP client secret and run the exchange.

view.caption: The Auth Service redirects the client to the external IdP, exchanges the one-time code server-to-server for an id token, and maps the identity in the User / Credential Store.

options.name: Implicit flow (token in redirect)
options.description: The IdP returns the access token directly in the redirect URL fragment, with no server-side code exchange step.
pros: Fewer round trips: the IdP returns the token directly in the redirect fragment, no back-channel exchange.
Simpler to implement for a pure browser SPA with no server component.
No client secret to store on the Auth service.
cons: The access token lands in the URL fragment, exposed to browser history, logs, and referrer headers.
No way to safely deliver a refresh token, so sessions are short or re-prompt often.
Deprecated by OAuth 2.1 in favor of code + PKCE precisely because of these leaks.

view.caption: The client app redirects to the external IdP with response_type=token and the IdP returns the access_token directly in the redirect fragment.

options.name: Resource-owner password grant
options.description: The user types their IdP password into your app, which POSTs it straight to the IdP's token endpoint in exchange for a token.
pros: No redirect dance: the user types their IdP password into your app and you POST it for a token.
Trivial for a first-party CLI or legacy migration where redirects are awkward.
Single round trip to the token endpoint.
cons: Your app sees the user's IdP password directly, defeating the whole point of federation.
Cannot support MFA, consent screens, or social IdPs that never expose a password API.
Also removed in OAuth 2.1 — unsuitable for third-party or untrusted clients.

view.caption: The client app sends the username and password to the Auth Service, which POSTs them straight to the external IdP's token endpoint as a password grant.

steps.title: 4. Token Refresh & Revocation
steps.description: Resolve the stateless-token tension: issue short-lived access tokens plus a long-lived, rotating refresh token. Revocation is handled by short access TTLs and a denylist checked on refresh (and optionally per-request for high-value actions).
steps.decisionPrompt: A user clicks 'log out everywhere' but their access token is a stateless JWT valid for 15 more minutes. Now what?

concepts.definition: Each refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one.
concepts.whyItMatters: Detects token theft — a reused old refresh token signals compromise.

concepts.definition: A small store of revoked token/session ids checked on refresh.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes stateless tokens revocable without per-request lookups everywhere.

deepDives.title: Bounding stolen-token damage
points: Short access TTL (minutes) bounds how long a stolen access token works.
Rotate refresh tokens; a replayed old one means theft -> revoke the whole chain.
Keep a denylist of revoked session ids; check it on refresh and for sensitive operations.

traps.trap: Long-lived access tokens so 'log out' doesn't really log out
traps.why: The token keeps working until expiry regardless of logout.
traps.instead: Short access TTL + refresh; revoke the refresh token and denylist the session.

traps.why: A stolen refresh token grants indefinite access and theft is undetectable.
traps.instead: Rotate on every use; detect reuse of a retired refresh token as compromise.

failureDrills.scenario: A refresh token is stolen and used in parallel with the real user.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The theft is detected and access cut off.
failureDrills.mitigation: Rotation makes the old token reuse detectable; reuse triggers revocation of the chain.

strong: Short access + rotating refresh + denylist; detects refresh reuse.
Explains how logout actually takes effect.

recap.before: Stateless tokens couldn't be revoked before expiry.
recap.after: Short access TTL + rotating refresh + denylist make revocation real.
recap.newRisk: A denylist reintroduces a small stateful check; keep it cheap.

whyNow: Revocation is a hard requirement that the stateless model otherwise can't meet.

flows.name: Refresh with rotation + reuse detection
flows.note: A refresh token is checked against the denylist; a valid one rotates (old invalidated, new issued); a reused/retired one signals theft and revokes the whole chain.

view.caption: The client app refreshes at the Token Service, which checks the Revocation List denylist, rotates and signs via the Signing Keys / JWKS, and returns a new access token.

options.name: Short-lived JWT + refresh + denylist (default)
options.description: Access tokens are short-lived signed JWTs verified locally; a stored, rotating refresh token mints new ones, and a small denylist invalidates revoked tokens before they expire.
pros: Access tokens are stateless and short (~15 min), so most requests verify by signature with no DB hit.
Refresh tokens are stored and revocable, so 'log out everywhere' invalidates the refresh chain immediately.
A small denylist of revoked-but-unexpired access tokens covers the gap until natural expiry.
cons: An already-issued access token stays valid until expiry unless it's checked against the denylist.
Sensitive endpoints must consult the revocation list, reintroducing a lookup on the hot path.
Refresh-token rotation and reuse detection add bookkeeping.

view.caption: The client refreshes at the Token Service, which checks the Revocation List, then rotates and signs a new access token via the Signing Keys / JWKS.

options.name: Stateful opaque sessions (lookup every request)
options.description: Every request presents an opaque session id that the gateway looks up in a session store treated as the single source of truth for validity.
pros: Revocation is instant and total: delete the session row and every device is logged out on the next request.
No window where a stolen token stays valid — the session store is the single source of truth.
Tokens carry no claims, so leaking one reveals nothing without the store.
cons: Every request hits the session store, so it must be a fast, highly-available, horizontally-scaled cache.
That store becomes a central dependency and a potential bottleneck at 100k req/s.
Loses the stateless-verification benefit that lets edge/API nodes validate locally.

view.caption: Every request carries an opaque id to the API Gateway, which looks it up in the Session Store as the single source of truth.

options.name: Long-lived JWT, no revocation
options.description: The client holds one long-lived signed JWT that every service trusts purely by signature, with no refresh endpoint, session store, or denylist.
pros: Simplest possible model: verify the signature and trust the claims, zero state.
No revocation list, no session store, no refresh endpoint to operate.
Lowest per-request cost — pure signature check at the edge.
cons: 'Log out everywhere' and account compromise have no remedy until the token expires.
A leaked token is usable for its full (long) lifetime by anyone.
Forces a choice between long exposure windows and constant re-authentication.

view.caption: The client presents one long-lived token to the API Gateway, which verifies it purely against the Signing Keys / JWKS with no denylist.

steps.title: 5. Authorization (RBAC / ABAC)
steps.description: Authentication says who you are; authorization says what you may do. A policy service decides access from roles (RBAC) and/or attributes (ABAC). The decision needs real context — subject, action, resource, tenant, resource owner, and request attributes — not just a subject and an action string. Decisions are evaluated centrally and cached with a short TTL, stamped with a policy_version so a role or policy change invalidates stale entries. Enforce at the gateway and defense-in-depth at the resource.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you make the access decision, and how do you keep it fast on every request?

concepts.definition: Permissions granted via roles assigned to principals.
Decisions from attributes (owner == user, department, time).
concepts.whyItMatters: Expresses fine-grained, contextual rules RBAC can't.

options.name: Central policy service + cached decisions (default)
options.description: The gateway asks a policy service 'may this principal do this action?'; the service evaluates roles/attributes against the policy store, with decisions cached on the hot path.
pros: One place to define and audit policy.
Cache decisions/roles to keep the hot path fast.
cons: A dependency on the request path; cache staleness on permission change.

view.caption: The API Gateway asks the Authorization Service for a decision, which it evaluates against the Policy / Roles Store and caches.

options.name: Embed permissions in the token
options.description: Roles and permissions are baked into the access token's claims at issuance, so the gateway reads them off the token without any authorization lookup.
pros: No authz lookup — claims travel with the token.
cons: Stale until the token expires; a revoked role still works.
Token bloat for many permissions.

view.caption: The API Gateway reads roles and permissions directly off the token's claims at the Token Service, with no authorization lookup.

deepDives.title: Decision context, caching, and invalidation
points: The decision request must carry subject, action, resource, tenant_id, resource_owner, and request attributes (env, sensitivity, MFA satisfied) — a bare sub+action can't express ownership or tenant isolation.
Cache the allow/deny on the hot path with a short TTL (seconds) and stamp it with the policy_version it was decided under.

Invalidate on change: bumping a principal's or tenant's policy_version (role grant/revoke, policy edit) makes cached decisions for that key stale immediately; high-sensitivity actions skip the cache and re-evaluate against current policy.
Tenant isolation: every decision is scoped by tenant_id so one tenant's grants can never authorize another tenant's resources.

traps.trap: Enforcing authorization only at the gateway
traps.why: A bug or internal call that bypasses the gateway then has no check.
traps.instead: Defense in depth: gateway plus a check at the resource/service.

traps.trap: Baking coarse permissions into long-lived tokens
traps.why: Revoking a role doesn't take effect until the token expires.
traps.instead: Evaluate sensitive authz against current policy (or short token TTL).

failureDrills.scenario: An admin revokes a user's 'editor' role.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The user loses edit access promptly.
failureDrills.mitigation: Authz checks current policy (or token TTL is short); cache invalidation on role change.

strong: Separates authn from authz; central policy with caching and defense in depth.

recap.before: We knew who the user was but not what they could do.
recap.after: A policy service decides access from roles/attributes, enforced in depth.
recap.newRisk: Authz is on the hot path; caching vs freshness must be balanced.

whyNow: Identity is incomplete without deciding permissions.

view.caption: The API Gateway asks the Authorization Service 'may sub do action?'; it evaluates the Policy / Roles Store, returns allow/deny, and the gateway then calls the Resource Service.

steps.title: 6. Multi-Factor Authentication
steps.description: Add a second factor so a stolen password isn't enough. After password verification, require a TOTP/push/SMS challenge before issuing full tokens. Step-up MFA can also gate high-value actions specifically.
steps.decisionPrompt: A password is correct but the account has MFA. What do you issue, and when?

concepts.definition: Something you have (TOTP app, security key) or are, beyond the password.
concepts.whyItMatters: Defeats credential theft and stuffing — the password alone is insufficient.

concepts.definition: Requiring re-auth/MFA for sensitive operations even within a session.
concepts.whyItMatters: Protects high-value actions without forcing MFA on everything.

traps.trap: Issuing full tokens after the password, before MFA
traps.why: An attacker with the password is already in before the second factor is checked.
traps.instead: Issue only a limited 'MFA-pending' token until the second factor succeeds.

traps.trap: SMS as the only second factor
traps.why: SIM-swap and SS7 attacks make SMS the weakest factor.
traps.instead: Prefer TOTP/push/WebAuthn; treat SMS as a fallback, not the default.

failureDrills.scenario: An attacker has the correct password for an MFA account.
failureDrills.mitigation: Full tokens are withheld until the second factor verifies; failed MFA is rate-limited and alerted.

strong: Gates full tokens behind MFA and supports step-up.

recap.after: A second factor is required; high-value actions can step up.
recap.newRisk: Recovery flows and factor enrollment become new attack surfaces.

whyNow: MFA is the highest-leverage defense once the core auth flow exists.

flows.name: MFA-gated token issuance
flows.note: A correct password yields only a limited mfa_pending_token + challenge, never full tokens. Access + refresh are issued strictly after the second factor verifies; a failed factor returns 401 and is rate-limited.

view.caption: After the password is OK, the Auth Service invokes the MFA Service, which challenges via SMS / TOTP / Push; once verified, the Auth Service issues tokens at the Token Service.

options.name: TOTP authenticator app (default)
options.description: After the password verifies, the user enters a six-digit code their authenticator app derives offline from a shared seed, which the MFA service recomputes and compares.
pros: Codes are generated offline from a shared secret, so there's no per-login cost or SMS dependency.
Phishing-resistant against passive interception since codes rotate every 30 seconds.
Cheap and standard (RFC 6238) — any authenticator app works.
cons: Users must enroll a device and safely store backup codes for recovery.
The shared seed must be stored encrypted and is a juicy target if the DB leaks.
Real-time phishing proxies can still relay a live code.

view.caption: After the password is OK, the MFA Service challenges via the TOTP authenticator (SMS / TOTP / Push); on verification the Auth Service issues tokens at the Token Service.

options.name: SMS / push one-time code
options.description: After the password verifies, the MFA service sends a one-time code or approve/deny prompt to the user's phone via an SMS or push gateway.
pros: No app to install — works for any user with a phone, lowering enrollment friction.
Push variants give a one-tap approve/deny that's easy for non-technical users.
Good as a fallback factor when an authenticator is unavailable.
cons: SMS is vulnerable to SIM-swap and SS7 interception, and costs money per send.
Delivery latency and carrier failures create login friction and a delivery dependency.
Push fatigue: users reflexively approve prompts, enabling MFA-bombing attacks.

view.caption: After the password is OK, the MFA Service sends a one-time code or push via the SMS / Push Gateway; once verified the Auth Service issues tokens at the Token Service.

options.name: WebAuthn / passkey (phishing-resistant)
options.description: The MFA service issues a challenge that a hardware authenticator or passkey signs with an origin-bound private key; the secret never leaves the device.
pros: Public-key challenge bound to the origin, so credentials can't be phished or replayed to a fake site.
Hardware-backed (security key, platform TPM, passkey) and nothing secret leaves the device.
Can replace the password entirely for a passwordless flow.
cons: Requires browser/OS support and an authenticator the user has registered.
Account recovery and cross-device sync are harder to get right.
More involved to implement and enroll than a six-digit code.

view.caption: After the password is OK, the WebAuthn Verifier has the Authenticator / Security Key sign a challenge; on verification the Auth Service issues tokens at the Token Service.

steps.title: 7. Rate Limiting & Abuse Defense
steps.description: Public login endpoints invite brute-force and credential-stuffing. Rate-limit per account and per IP, add exponential lockout/backoff and CAPTCHA on suspicion, and detect distributed attacks — all while not locking out legitimate users.
steps.decisionPrompt: Attackers try millions of leaked passwords across many accounts from many IPs. How do you defend without locking out real users?

concepts.definition: Trying leaked username/password pairs at scale.
concepts.whyItMatters: The dominant real-world attack; per-IP limits alone don't stop distributed attempts.

concepts.definition: Hard account lock vs progressive slowdown.
concepts.whyItMatters: Hard locks enable denial-of-service against victims; prefer throttling + step-up.

deepDives.title: Defending without self-inflicted DoS
points: Throttle per account and per IP; add CAPTCHA/step-up on anomaly rather than hard-locking.
Detect distributed stuffing by global failure-rate patterns, not just per-IP counters.
Check credentials against known-breached password lists; alert and audit suspicious logins.

deepDives.title: Counter mechanics and abuse alerting
points: Counters live in a fast replicated store (e.g. Redis), keyed by dimension (account, IP, subnet, tenant, device) with per-window TTL expiry — never in the primary DB on the hot path.
Treat login throttling, MFA-prompt throttling, and account-recovery throttling as separate limits: each has a different abuse mode (stuffing vs MFA-bombing vs recovery hijack).
Counters are best-effort across regions; a small over-count from eventual consistency is acceptable — favor availability over a globally exact count.
Alert from the audit stream on refresh-token reuse, impossible-travel logins, credential-stuffing spikes (global failure rate), and signing-key anomalies.
Give legitimate users a recovery path after a suspicious-activity block (verified email/MFA step-up) so anomaly defense never becomes a permanent lockout.

traps.trap: Hard-locking an account after N failures
traps.why: An attacker can deliberately lock any victim out — a denial-of-service.
traps.instead: Throttle and challenge (CAPTCHA/step-up); reserve locks for clear compromise with recovery.

traps.why: Credential stuffing spreads across thousands of IPs, slipping under per-IP limits.
traps.instead: Add per-account limits and global anomaly detection on failure rates.

failureDrills.scenario: A botnet runs a slow, distributed stuffing attack.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It's detected and blunted without locking out legitimate users.
failureDrills.mitigation: Global failure-rate anomaly detection + per-account throttling + breached-password checks.

strong: Throttles + challenges instead of hard locks; detects distributed stuffing globally.

recap.before: Login was open to brute force and stuffing.
recap.after: Layered throttling, challenges, and anomaly detection blunt attacks.
recap.newRisk: Tuning to stop attackers without harming real users is ongoing.

whyNow: A correct auth flow is still exploitable without abuse defenses on the login edge.

view.caption: Login attempts pass through the Login Rate Limiter, which forwards within-limit traffic to the Auth Service, throttles or challenges the client, and records events in the Audit Log.

options.name: Adaptive multi-dimensional limiting (default)
options.description: A rate limiter counts failures per account, per IP, and per subnet, escalating suspicious traffic to a CAPTCHA/MFA challenge and feeding patterns to the audit log.
pros: Counts failures per account, per IP, and per IP-subnet so a botnet can't dodge a single counter.
Escalates to a CAPTCHA/MFA challenge instead of a hard lockout, so real users aren't locked out.
Feeds the audit log to surface credential-stuffing patterns across accounts.
cons: Needs a fast shared counter store (e.g. Redis) keyed on multiple dimensions.
Tuning thresholds is ongoing — too tight blocks users, too loose lets attacks through.
Distributed low-and-slow attacks under every threshold still require anomaly detection.

view.caption: Login attempts hit the multi-dimensional Login Rate Limiter, which forwards within-limit traffic to the Auth Service, throttles or challenges abusers, and feeds the Audit Log.

options.name: Per-account lockout after N failures
options.description: The limiter counts consecutive failed logins per account and locks that account for a cooldown once a threshold is crossed.
pros: Dead simple: count consecutive failures per account and lock for a cooldown.
Directly thwarts password-guessing against a single targeted account.
Easy to explain to users and to reason about.
cons: Becomes a denial-of-service vector: an attacker can lock out any victim by failing on purpose.
Useless against credential stuffing, which spreads one attempt across millions of accounts.
Per-account state alone misses IP- and network-level attack signals.

view.caption: Login attempts hit a Per-Account Lockout counter, which forwards within-limit traffic to the Auth Service and throttles or challenges the client.

options.name: Per-IP rate limit only
options.description: A single edge counter caps login attempts per source IP, throttling any address that exceeds the limit.
pros: Cheap single-dimension counter that blunts a single noisy source quickly.
No per-account state, so it scales trivially at the edge.
Effective against naive single-host scripts.
cons: Botnets and residential proxies spread requests across thousands of IPs, slipping under any per-IP cap.
Shared NAT/CGNAT means many real users behind one IP get throttled together.
Says nothing about which accounts are under attack, so the audit signal is weak.

view.caption: Login attempts hit a single Per-IP Rate Limiter, which forwards only within-limit traffic from each source IP to the Auth Service.

steps.title: 8. Scaling Verification & Key Rotation
steps.description: Make per-request verification scale platform-wide. Stateless tokens verify by signature using published public keys (JWKS), so any service checks them without a central call. Rotate signing keys regularly and keep the denylist small and replicated.
steps.decisionPrompt: Every one of 100k req/s must verify a credential. How do you do that without a central auth bottleneck?

concepts.definition: A published set of public keys verifiers fetch to validate token signatures.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets every service verify locally; key rotation is transparent to them.

concepts.definition: Periodically replacing signing keys, overlapping old+new during transition.
concepts.whyItMatters: Limits the blast radius of a key compromise.

concepts.definition: A header on each token naming which signing key signed it, matched against the published JWKS.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets verifiers pick the right public key during overlapping rotation and refetch JWKS on an unknown kid instead of failing blindly.

options.name: Local signature verification via JWKS + small denylist (default)
options.description: Every service verifies a token's signature locally using public keys fetched from the published JWKS, consulting a small replicated denylist only for sensitive operations.
pros: No central auth call per request — scales to the whole platform.
Key rotation is transparent via published JWKS.
cons: Revocation needs the denylist (small, replicated, checked for sensitive ops).
Key management discipline required.

view.caption: The API Gateway verifies each token's signature locally against the published Signing Keys / JWKS, with no central call.

options.name: Central introspection per request
options.description: The gateway calls the auth service to introspect each presented token on every request rather than verifying it locally.
pros: Instant revocation; central control.
cons: The auth service becomes a hot dependency on every request — a scaling and availability bottleneck.

view.caption: The API Gateway calls the Token Service to introspect every presented token rather than verifying it locally.

deepDives.title: Rotation and revocation at scale
points: Publish current + next signing keys via JWKS; verifiers cache the set and honor its max-age. Each token carries a kid header so a verifier picks the matching public key.
On an unknown kid, the verifier refetches JWKS once (cache miss) before rejecting — so a freshly rotated key is picked up without a deploy, but a forged kid still fails fast.
Overlap old and new keys during scheduled rotation so in-flight tokens signed by the old key stay valid until they expire.

Emergency rotation (suspected private-key leak): publish a new key, stop signing with the leaked kid, and remove it from JWKS so every token bearing that kid fails verification immediately — blast radius is every token signed by that key until it would have expired.
Keep the revocation denylist small (short access TTL means few live tokens) and replicate it close to verifiers.

Observe it: track JWKS fetch rate / cache-miss rate, verification failure rate by reason (bad sig, unknown kid, expired), and rotation events — these are the signals that a rotation or compromise is mid-flight.

traps.trap: Calling the auth service to introspect every request
traps.why: It becomes a single hot dependency that caps platform throughput and availability.
traps.instead: Verify signatures locally via JWKS; reserve central checks for sensitive operations.

traps.why: A single leaked key compromises every token indefinitely.
traps.instead: Rotate on a schedule with overlapping validity; revoke a key fast on compromise.

strong: Verifies locally via JWKS and rotates keys; keeps the denylist tiny.

recap.before: Verification risked a central bottleneck and keys were static.
recap.after: Local signature verification via rotating JWKS scales platform-wide with bounded revocation.
recap.newRisk: Key rotation and denylist replication are ongoing operational duties.

whyNow: Per-request verification is the hot path; it must scale without a central chokepoint.

view.caption: The API Gateway verifies a token's signature at the Token Service using public keys from the Signing Keys / JWKS, checks the Revocation List for sensitive ops, then calls the Resource Service.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end, read as four paths. Login path: limiter → auth → credential store / OIDC → MFA → token service, where full tokens are issued only after the second factor verifies. Refresh path: token service → hashed refresh-token family → rotation/reuse detection → signing keys. Request (hot) path: the gateway and resource verify the JWT locally against rotating JWKS keys, with a denylist check only for sensitive operations, then a cached RBAC/ABAC decision scoped by tenant. Operations path: every security-relevant event is audited, risk signals drive abuse alerts, and key rotation runs on a schedule with emergency revocation available. Salted slow-hashed passwords (or federated OAuth/OIDC) sit behind the rate-limited, abuse-aware login; short access tokens bound exposure and rotating refresh tokens plus the denylist make revocation real.

view.caption: The client app passes through the Login Rate Limiter to the Auth Service, which authenticates via the credential store, IdP, and MFA, issues JWKS-signed tokens, and the API Gateway then verifies them, checks revocation, authorizes against policy, and serves resources while auditing throughout.

functional.how: Short access token + rotating refresh (or session).
OAuth/OIDC code flow mapped to local users.
RBAC/ABAC policy service, enforced in depth.
Refresh rotation + denylist; short access TTL.

nonFunctional.how: Local JWKS signature verification, no central call.
Short access TTL + denylist checked on refresh/sensitive ops.
Layered rate limits, MFA, refresh rotation, breached-password checks.
Immutable audit log of auth and permission events.
Stateless tokens + rotating JWKS verified locally.

say: Restate it: prove identity (authn) and decide permissions (authz), securely at scale.
List functional (login, federation, authz, MFA, revoke) and non-functional (safe storage, fast verify, revocation, abuse resistance).
Lead with the security stance: credential storage and revocation are make-or-break.
Note verification rate dwarfs login rate — optimize the per-request path.
Sketch login, refresh, logout, and an internal authorize check.
Start with salted slow-hash login, then sessions-vs-tokens, then OAuth/OIDC.
Add refresh + revocation, RBAC/ABAC authorization, then MFA and abuse defense.
Go deep where pushed: token revocation, OIDC validation, or stuffing defense.
Verify locally via rotating JWKS; keep the denylist small.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant risks (credential theft, stale revocation).

expectations: Salts and slow-hashes passwords; uses generic login errors.
Knows a token/session is issued after login and presented per request.
Articulates sessions vs stateless tokens and the revocation tradeoff.
Implements short access + rotating refresh with a denylist; validates OIDC tokens fully.
Separates authn from authz with RBAC/ABAC.
Scales verification with local JWKS + key rotation, avoiding central introspection.
Designs distributed credential-stuffing defense without self-inflicted DoS.
Reasons about blast radius of key/credential compromise and revocation latency.

followUps: How would you implement passwordless / WebAuthn (passkeys) and where does it change the flow?
How do you design secure account recovery without it becoming the weakest link?
How would you support single sign-on (SSO) and single logout across many apps?
How do you handle service-to-service auth (mTLS / workload identity) distinct from user auth?
How would you detect and respond to a leaked signing key in production?
How do you support multi-tenant isolation so one tenant's tokens can't access another's resources?

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework
links.why: The core standard behind delegated authorization and OAuth flows.

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: OpenID Connect Core
links.why: Adds identity, ID tokens, and user authentication semantics on top of OAuth.

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: Web Authentication: An API for Accessing Public Key Credentials
links.why: Primary reference for passkeys, phishing-resistant MFA, and public-key authentication.

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: NIST Digital Identity Guidelines
links.why: Useful for authentication assurance, session lifecycle, MFA, and identity proofing.

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: Authentication Cheat Sheet
links.why: Practical security reference for login, password storage, lockout, and session handling.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Redis Client-Side Caching
links.why: Shows cache invalidation and coherence mechanics for low-latency read paths.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
links.why: Grounds monitoring and alerting decisions in user-visible reliability signals.
